marcus_drusus_convo_0_n01.npcDialogue: You're the one who listens — that's what they tell me, and gods know I've had enough of men who only wait for their turn to speak. I'll be direct. I have a friend in Asculum — a man named Gaius, who fought beside my father at Numantia — and his sons cannot vote on the conscription laws that will send them to die. Two centuries of shared blood, and Rome still calls them foreigners. My bill ends that. So tell me where you stand, because I haven't got time for the ones who need convincing.

options.playerDialogue: The allies have earned it ten times over. I'm with you.
The senate will bury this bill and everyone attached to it. Have you planned for that?
When the citizenship passes, the entire patronage map redraws. I'd like to understand what that looks like.

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n02.npcDialogue: Good. Then you see what most of them refuse to — that these people aren't petitioners, they're partners we've been cheating. I've spent three years stitching this coalition together, calling in every favor I've ever held, and I can feel the thread pulling tight. What I need now aren't more believers. I need voices the senate cannot simply dismiss as provincial sentiment.

options.playerDialogue: How did you hold a coalition like that together? Who's in it?

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n03.npcDialogue: Of course they'll try — I expect nothing less from men who've made careers of saying no. But I have equestrians who want the allied trade routes opened, plebeians who remember that their grandfathers and the Marsic grandfathers bled in the same ditch at Arausio, even optimates — a few — who understand that a republic which betrays the men who defend it is already dead. The senate can obstruct. It cannot un-ring this bell.

options.playerDialogue: And if they find a quieter way to stop you? Something a veto can't undo?
I hope that's true. What could someone like me do for the cause?

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n04.npcDialogue: You're sharper than you let on — most men your age wouldn't ask that so directly. Yes. When the Italians enter the tribes, the equestrian courts reorganize, new client networks form overnight, and every man who positioned himself on the right side of this beforehand finds doors swinging open that were bricked shut a month ago. So — is that the kind of help you're offering, or just the kind of question you're asking?

options.playerDialogue: Call me practical rather than cynical. What do you need done?
That's exactly the kind of help I'm offering. Let's talk specifics.

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n05.npcDialogue: Here is the harder question — the one that separates the sympathizers from the committed. Will you stand for this at the rostra? Not in back rooms, not in careful whispers over dinner — at the rostra, in daylight, where the optimates can see your face and write your name down. Quiet support keeps you safe. Public support changes what you are.

options.playerDialogue: I'll speak for it publicly. Let them write my name wherever they like.
I'm more useful to you in the shadows — a broker, not a target.
Let me see how the senate moves first. I won't expose myself blindly.

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n06.npcDialogue: Caution. I know it well — I've watched it paralyze better men than either of us. The senate has ears in every portico. But let me look at you a moment — let me see what's behind the prudence.

responses.playerDialogue: My hesitation is discipline, not cowardice. I don't lend my name to positions I haven't weighed to the last grain.
I want to help — truly. But if I burn my bridges to the other side, I lose the ability to broker any kind of peace.
Name the key opponents. I'll have their minds changed before the vote — then I'll step into the light.
A man the optimates think is neutral can serve your cause better than any declared ally. That's not hedging — that's positioning.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I'm watching. I'm weighing. That's not nothing.

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n07.npcDialogue: Good. That's what I needed to hear. But there's one more thing — and it matters.

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n08.npcDialogue: Shadow work has its uses — I won't pretend otherwise. Here is what I'd ask of you: the optimates are organizing their opposition, and I need to know how. What they say behind closed doors, who's wavering, where the knives are being sharpened. You move in those rooms. Will you be my eyes there?

options.playerDialogue: I can do that. What do I get in return?
Freely given — no ledger, no debt. Some things are simply worth doing.
I won't carry whispers against men who've trusted me. That's a line I don't cross.

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n09.npcDialogue: You have standing with the optimates — real standing, not just proximity. That makes you valuable and, frankly, complicated. I need a back-channel to Catulus. Someone he trusts, someone who can make him see that this bill doesn't have to mean the end of everything he's built his life around. Could you be that bridge?

options.playerDialogue: I can try. Catulus is proud, but he's not beyond reason — not yet.
My relationships aren't currency — not even for a cause I believe in.

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n10.npcDialogue: Then we understand each other. Welcome to whatever this is — I won't call it a cause, because causes sound noble and this will mostly be ugly. I can't promise you safety. I can't even promise we'll win. But what we're doing is right, and Rome will remember the men who stood here — one way or another, she'll remember.

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n11.npcDialogue: I understand — a cautious man with a conscience. Those are rarer than you'd think. Keep your eyes open, Crispus. When the vote comes — and it will come, one way or another — I'll know whether you were with us in the ways that mattered. The quiet ways count too. I've just learned not to rely on them.

marcus_drusus_convo_0_n12.npcDialogue: Then I know where you stand — or rather, where you won't. Go carefully. In my experience, men who refuse to choose a side wake up one morning to find the side has been chosen for them, and rarely to their liking. I hope that isn't you. I genuinely hope it isn't.

deltas.reason: Publicly committed to Drusus's reform coalition
Seen as an ally of Italian citizenship
Marked as a reformer by the conservative bloc
Chose the generous interpretation of Roman duty
Word reaches the optimates that you declined to help Drusus
Maintained deniability at a critical moment
